items.title: White House issues AI innovation and security executive order
items.summary: The White House issued an executive order establishing a voluntary framework for advanced AI developers to give the federal government pre-release access for security assessments.
items.details: The June 2, 2026 order, titled "Promoting Advanced Artificial Intelligence Innovation and Security," directs multiple agencies to strengthen federal cyber defenses and establish an AI cybersecurity clearinghouse. It is described as the administration's third major AI executive order of the term.

items.title: President signs additional executive orders in late June
items.summary: The President signed further executive orders on June 22 and June 25, according to the White House record of presidential actions.
items.details: The White House Presidential Actions page lists executive orders dated June 22 and June 25, 2026, along with proclamations and presidential memoranda issued June 29. The Federal Register records 42 executive orders signed in 2026 through EO 14413.

items.title: Administration issues national security fact sheets
items.summary: The White House published national security materials in late June, including a fact sheet on securing the nation against advanced cryptographic attacks.
items.details: On June 22, 2026, the White House released a fact sheet titled "President Donald J. Trump Secures the Nation Against Advanced Cryptographic Attacks" and a message marking the anniversary of the Battle of Okinawa as part of its national security priorities page.

items.title: U.S. and Iran agree on roadmap toward final deal
items.summary: U.S. and Iranian negotiators approved a roadmap aimed at reaching a final deal within 60 days and creating a mechanism to end hostilities in Lebanon.
items.details: During June 21-22 talks in Switzerland, the parties agreed to establish a "High Level Committee" for oversight and working groups on nuclear, sanctions, and dispute resolution. They also agreed to reopen the Strait of Hormuz toll-free for at least 60 days and to a Lebanon deconfliction cell.

items.title: Dispute over nuclear inspections complicates U.S.-Iran talks
items.summary: The U.S. and Iran differed over whether Tehran had agreed to allow U.N. inspectors to view bombed Iranian nuclear sites as mediators worked toward a permanent end to the war.
items.details: As of June 23, technical teams worked on details following the Switzerland talks, and ship traffic through the Strait of Hormuz was increasing. Data firm Kpler recorded 39 ship crossings on one day, below the roughly 100 daily crossings seen before the war.

items.title: House considers War Powers measure on Lebanon
items.summary: A resolution directing the removal of U.S. Armed Forces from hostilities in Lebanon was listed on the House floor schedule.
items.details: According to Congress.gov, the House floor activity for June 30, 2026, included a measure invoking Section 5(c) of the War Powers Resolution to remove United States Armed Forces from hostilities in Lebanon.

items.title: Congress passes major housing affordability bill
items.summary: Congress passed a large housing affordability bill; the President canceled a planned signing ceremony, citing a request that Congress first pass a voter ID bill.
items.details: The House cleared the final vote on the bill on June 23, 2026. Provisions include streamlining certain federal financing regulations, a grant program for preapproved housing designs, and changes to manufactured home requirements. The President canceled the June signing ceremony.

items.title: House Rules reviews Fiscal Year 2027 defense authorization
items.summary: The House Rules Committee reviewed the National Defense Authorization Act for Fiscal Year 2027 and its more than 1,300 amendments.
items.details: According to GovTrack, the Monday, June 29 Rules Committee meeting was set to review H.R. 8800, the NDAA for FY2027, and its 1,363 amendments. The Senate was out of session that week while the House remained in session.

items.title: Senate appropriators continue FY2027 spending negotiations
items.summary: Senate appropriators continued negotiations over a topline spending agreement for their fiscal year 2027 spending bills.
items.details: According to the National Low Income Housing Coalition, disagreements over topline amounts for defense and non-defense programs affected the Senate Appropriations Committee's work in June 2026. The House Appropriations Committee held a June 3 markup of its FY2027 Transportation and Housing bill.

items.title: Supreme Court rules on birthright citizenship
items.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that the Constitution guarantees automatic birthright citizenship to virtually all children born in the United States, rejecting the executive order on the issue.
items.details: In a 6-3 decision issued June 30, Chief Justice John Roberts wrote the opinion. The executive order had sought to bar citizenship for babies born in the U.S. to parents who entered illegally or who hold temporary visas; it never took effect.

items.title: Supreme Court rules on presidential removal power
items.summary: The Supreme Court found that the President's firing of a Federal Trade Commissioner without cause was lawful, further narrowing a longstanding precedent.
items.details: In a 6-3 ruling issued June 29 in Trump v. Slaughter, the court addressed the March 2025 removal of FTC Commissioner Rebecca Kelly Slaughter. In a separate matter, the court ruled 5-4 that the Federal Reserve's Lisa Cook can remain in her position for now.

items.title: Supreme Court rules on transgender athletes in school sports
items.summary: The Supreme Court ruled that states may ban transgender girls from participating in sports at publicly funded schools.
items.details: Justice Brett Kavanaugh wrote the majority opinion in the June 30 decision, which involved cases at the college and high school levels. The ruling follows a prior decision upholding state laws restricting gender-affirming care for minors.

items.title: Supreme Court decides mail ballot and geofence cases
items.summary: The Supreme Court issued decisions on the counting of late-arriving mail ballots and on law enforcement use of geofence searches.
items.details: In decisions issued June 29, the justices addressed the legality of counting late-arriving mail ballots and ruled that law enforcement must obtain warrants for "geofence" searches. Justice Elena Kagan wrote a 6-3 majority opinion in the geofence case.

items.title: Supreme Court clears Texas map for 2026 midterms
items.summary: The Supreme Court cleared the way for Texas to use its congressional map for the 2026 midterms while litigation continues.
items.details: A federal court in El Paso had barred the map in November 2025, finding it a racial gerrymander. The Supreme Court stayed that ruling and formalized the reversal by summary order on April 27, 2026, allowing the new map, which is expected to affect five seats.

items.title: Redistricting activity continues across multiple states
items.summary: Multiple states enacted or contested new congressional maps ahead of the 2026 midterms, with additional efforts anticipated for 2028.
items.details: According to Democracy Docket, states including Texas, North Carolina, Missouri, Ohio, Florida, Tennessee, Alabama, and Louisiana enacted new maps. California voters authorized redraws, and state courts in Utah ordered a new map.

items.title: Colorado holds gubernatorial primaries
items.summary: Colorado held its Democratic and Republican gubernatorial primaries on June 30, 2026, to select nominees for the November election.
items.details: Incumbent Democratic Governor Jared Polis is term-limited. Candidates qualified for the primary ballot either by petition or by achieving 30% at the party state assembly, according to Ballotpedia.

CA.title: Newsom signs 2026-27 state budget
CA.summary: Governor Gavin Newsom signed California's 2026-27 state budget, described by his office as a balanced spending plan with no deficit.
CA.details: The June 29 signing included budget bills such as AB 109 and SB 110. Earlier in the week, the Governor and legislative leaders announced agreements including the Veterans and Affordable Housing Bond Act of 2026.

CO.title: Polis signs natural medicine bill
CO.summary: Governor Jared Polis signed HB26-1325, which specifies requirements for rules governing the administration of ibogaine.
CO.details: Signed June 4, 2026, the bill allows use of semi-synthetic ibogaine if approved by the board as a natural medicine and changes the composition of the Natural Medicine Advisory Board.
CO.source: Colorado Division of Professions and Occupations

CT.title: Lamont signs youth online safety and AI law
CT.summary: Governor Ned Lamont signed Public Act 26-15, establishing youth online safety protections, AI regulations, and workforce training initiatives.
CT.details: The bipartisan law was developed with the Attorney General and State Senator James Maroney. Officials said it addresses youth social media use and AI, and includes workforce training provisions for technology jobs.

IL.title: New Illinois laws take effect July 1
IL.summary: A group of new Illinois laws took effect July 1, 2026, including a measure suspending an inflation adjustment to the motor fuel tax rate.
IL.details: Public Act 104-0468, signed in June 2026, suspends the Consumer Price Index adjustment for the motor fuel tax rate until 2027. Other laws address cyberbullying definitions and driver's license renewal for seniors.

NH.title: Ayotte signs 54 bills into law
NH.summary: Governor Kelly Ayotte signed 54 bills into law, according to a June 19 announcement, and issued several vetoes.
NH.details: The signed bills included measures on health benefit plan claims data and school administrative unit budgets. The Governor also issued veto statements on bills concerning historical markers and risk pool operations.

OH.title: DeWine signs bills and issues election-law veto
OH.summary: Governor Mike DeWine signed several bills into law and vetoed House Bill 472, which addressed photo identification for mail-in ballots.
OH.details: In his June 24 veto message, DeWine stated the bill would not add security and would create a burden for mail voters. He also signed measures including Senate Bill 19 on academic intervention services and Senate Bill 106 on electric vehicle charging stations.

LA.title: Landry signs consumer data privacy law
LA.summary: Governor Jeff Landry signed the Louisiana Data Privacy Act, making Louisiana the twenty-second state with a comprehensive privacy law.
LA.details: Senate Bill 386 was signed May 29, 2026, and takes effect January 1, 2027. The enacted version removed explicit "artificial intelligence system" language present in an earlier draft. It is enforced by the Attorney General.

VA.title: U.S. Supreme Court declines Virginia redistricting appeal
VA.summary: The U.S. Supreme Court rejected Virginia Democrats' emergency appeal over congressional redistricting, leaving the 2021 map in place.
VA.details: The Virginia Supreme Court struck down a voter-approved redistricting referendum in a 4-3 May 8 ruling on procedural grounds. The U.S. Supreme Court rejected the appeal on May 15, and the state's current map, giving Democrats a 6-5 advantage, remains for 2026.

AZ.title: Hobbs vetoes AI bills
AZ.summary: Governor Katie Hobbs vetoed three AI-related bills passed by the legislature, part of a larger batch of vetoes.
AZ.details: The bills, vetoed June 19, included HB 2311, a chatbot safety measure requiring disclosure that a user is interacting with AI, and HB 2592, which addressed state agency AI adoption. They were among 88 bills vetoed in a single day.

IA.title: Reynolds signs bills including age-verification measure
IA.summary: Governor Kim Reynolds signed a list of bills into law on June 1, 2026, including a requirement for age verification on certain websites.
IA.details: The signed bills included HF 864 on age verification for sites with material pornographic for minors and HF 2231 establishing a seal of civics excellence program. The governor had until June 2 to act on remaining bills.

disclaimer: This digest reports verifiable developments from cited sources and does not endorse any party, official, or position.
